does: This addendum documents GPT-5's enhancements in managing sensitive conversational contexts, introducing new evaluation frameworks for measuring emotional dependence, mental health support quality, and resistance to adversarial prompts designed to circumvent safety guidelines. The paper presents benchmarks that assess how well the model maintains appropriate boundaries when users seek emotional support or mental health guidance, alongside metrics for detecting and refusing jailbreak attempts across various attack vectors.
connects: This work builds on prior system card documentation practices pioneered by OpenAI and others, extending earlier safety evaluation methodologies developed for GPT-3 and GPT-4. It responds to growing concerns about AI assistants inadvertently replacing human mental health professionals, informed by research on AI alignment, robustness testing, and adversarial prompting techniques. The emotional reliance benchmarks connect to broader work in AI ethics examining appropriate use cases and user vulnerability, while jailbreak resistance metrics draw from ongoing efforts in prompt injection prevention and adversarial robustness in large language models.
enables: This work establishes baseline expectations and evaluation standards for how future large language models should handle sensitive conversations, enabling more rigorous safety assessment during development cycles. The introduced benchmarks could facilitate better deployment practices, allowing organizations to measure compliance with ethical guidelines before release. These standards may also inform regulatory frameworks for AI systems, particularly those likely to interact with vulnerable populations seeking mental health support or emotional validation, and create foundations for industry-wide safety consensus on what constitutes responsible behavior in high-stakes conversational domains.
